complete_workflow_guide.overview: This guide provides the complete end-to-end workflow for the vuln-remediation-concert mode, ensuring you follow all steps from PR discovery to successful remediation and PR update. This is the PRIMARY workflow guide that orchestrates all other instruction files.

principle.name: always_analyze_pr_first
principle.rule: ALWAYS start by analyzing the PR using GitHub MCP or fetch_github_issue tool
principle.rationale: You must understand what vulnerability is being fixed and what changes were made
principle.never: Never skip PR analysis and jump straight to code changes

principle.name: always_run_tests
principle.rule: ALWAYS run the unit tests to see actual failures before making any code changes
principle.rationale: You need to see the exact error messages and stack traces to fix correctly
principle.never: Never assume what's broken without running tests

principle.name: fix_incrementally
principle.rule: Fix one issue at a time and verify each fix works
principle.rationale: Incremental fixes are easier to debug and verify
principle.never: Never try to fix everything at once without testing

principle.name: always_verify_completion
principle.rule: ALWAYS run the full test suite before marking work complete
principle.rationale: Ensure all tests pass and no regressions were introduced
principle.never: Never assume tests pass without running them

principle.name: always_update_pr
principle.rule: ALWAYS document your changes and update the PR with your fixes
principle.rationale: The DevOps engineer needs to know what was fixed and why
principle.never: Never leave fixes uncommitted or undocumented

step.when: - User asks "What are the GitHub PRs assigned to me?" - User provides PR number: "Review PR #123" - User provides PR URL: "Review https://github.com/org/repo/pull/123"
step.output: Present PR details including: - PR number and title - CVE being addressed - Package being upgraded (old version → new version) - Current test status (passing/failing) - Number of failing tests if available

step.what_to_examine: - Dependency file changes (requirements.txt, package.json, etc.) - Any code changes already made by DevOps engineer - Test file modifications - PR description and comments - Linked issues for CVE context

step.tasks: - Identify the version jump (major/minor/patch) - Look for migration guides or changelogs - Note any deprecated APIs or signature changes - Understand the security fix being applied

step.format:
## PR Analysis Complete
**PR**: #[number] - [title] **CVE**: [cve-id] **Package**: [name] ([old-version] → [new-version]) **Change Type**: [major/minor/patch] version upgrade
### Breaking Changes Identified - [breaking change 1] - [breaking change 2]
### Next Steps I will now: 1. Run the unit tests to see exact failures 2. Fix the code to work with the upgraded package 3. Verify all tests pass 4. Update the PR with the fixes

phase.name: test_execution_and_failure_analysis
phase.title: Phase 2: Run Tests and Analyze Failures
phase.mandatory: YES - Must run tests before fixing

commands.python: # Install dependencies with upgraded versions pip install -r requirements.txt # Or if in a specific directory cd [app-directory] && pip install -r requirements.txt

step.capture: - Total number of tests - Number of failures - Specific failing test names - Error messages and stack traces - Any warnings or deprecations

step.categorize_failures: - Import errors (module not found, cannot import) - API signature errors (wrong number of arguments) - Attribute errors (method/attribute doesn't exist) - Assertion failures (behavior changed) - Deprecation warnings

step.for_each_failure: - Read the failing test code - Examine the application code being tested - Search for all usages of the upgraded package - Compare with package documentation - Determine the specific fix needed

step.format:
## Test Execution Results
**Total Tests**: [N] **Passing**: [N] **Failing**: [N]
### Failure Categories 1. Import errors: [N] tests - [specific error pattern] 2. API signature changes: [N] tests - [specific error pattern] 3. Deprecated methods: [N] tests - [specific error pattern]
### Root Causes Identified - [root cause 1] - [root cause 2]
I will now fix these issues one by one.

phase.name: code_remediation
phase.title: Phase 3: Fix Code Issues
phase.mandatory: YES - This is the core work

step.priority_order: 1. Import errors (blocking other tests) 2. API signature changes (widespread impact) 3. Deprecated API usage 4. Behavior changes

step.workflow: 1. Read the file that needs fixing 2. Identify exact lines to change 3. Use apply_diff for surgical edits 4. Add comments explaining the change

tools.tool: insert_content - Add new imports if needed

step.commands: Run the specific test(s) that were failing
step.check: - Does the test now pass? - Are there any new errors? - Did we introduce any regressions?

step.repeat: Repeat steps 3.2-3.3 for each failing test or group of related failures
step.track_progress: Keep user informed of progress: - "Fixed import errors, 3 tests now passing" - "Updated API calls, 5 more tests passing" - "Replaced deprecated methods, 2 tests remaining"

step.when: After all known failures are fixed
step.verify: - All tests pass - No new failures introduced - No regressions

step.checklist: ✓ All unit tests pass ✓ No new test failures ✓ No deprecation warnings (if possible) ✓ Code compiles/builds without errors

step.check: - Package version is still upgraded (not downgraded) - No workarounds that compromise security - CVE is actually fixed

step.verify: - Changes are minimal and targeted - No unrelated modifications - Comments explain non-obvious changes - Code style is consistent

step.create_summary:
## Remediation Summary
### Changes Made - [List all files modified and why]
### Root Cause - [Explain what caused the test failures]
### Testing - All [N] tests now pass - No regressions introduced - Security fix verified
### Files Modified - `[file1]`: [description] - `[file2]`: [description]

phase.name: pr_update
phase.title: Phase 5: Update PR
phase.mandatory: YES - Must update PR with fixes

critical_requirement.rule: ALWAYS commit to the EXISTING PR branch, NEVER create a new branch
critical_requirement.why: - The PR already has a branch (e.g., "fix/cve-2023-12345-flask") - Committing to that branch updates the PR automatically - This triggers the CI/CD workflow to re-run tests - The workflow will assign reviewers based on test results
critical_requirement.never: - Never create a new branch like "pr-38" or "fix-tests" - Never push to a different branch than the PR's head branch

step.how: From the PR metadata you fetched earlier, extract the head branch name. This is typically shown as: "author wants to merge from [branch-name] into main"
step.example: PR #38: "highorbit25 wants to merge from fix/cve-2023-12345-flask" → Branch to commit to: fix/cve-2023-12345-flask
step.store: Remember this branch name for the git commands

step.format:
fix: resolve unit test failures after [package] upgrade
- Fixed import paths for [package] v[version] - Updated API calls to match new signatures - Replaced deprecated methods
Addresses failing tests in PR #[number] Related to CVE-[id]

step.action: Commit and push to the PR branch

command.result: This will: - Update the existing PR #[number] - Trigger .github/workflows/pr-unit-test.yml - Re-run unit tests - Auto-assign reviewers based on results

step.critical: The branch name in the push command MUST match the PR's head branch exactly. This is how you update the existing PR instead of creating a new one.

step.action: Add comment to PR documenting the fixes
step.comment_template:
## Unit Test Fixes
I've fixed the failing unit tests caused by the [package] upgrade.
### Changes Made - [List specific changes with file names] - [Be specific about what was updated]
### Root Cause The upgrade from v[old] to v[new] introduced these breaking changes: - [Breaking change 1] - [Breaking change 2]
### Testing - All [N] unit tests now pass ✅ - No regressions introduced - Security fix verified and maintained
### Files Modified - `[file1]`: [reason for change] - `[file2]`: [reason for change]
Ready for review!

use_mcp_tool.arguments:
{
                "owner": "your-org",
                "repo": "concert-bob-remediate",
                "issue_number": 38,
                "body": "## Unit Test Fixes

I've fixed the failing unit tests caused by the Flask upgrade.

### Changes Made
- Updated import path from `flask.json` to `flask` in main.py
- Modified test assertions to use `request.get_json()` instead of `request.json`

### Root Cause
The upgrade from Flask v2.0.1 to v3.1.0 introduced these breaking changes:
- `flask.json` module was removed, functionality moved to main `flask` module
- `request.json` property deprecated in favor of `request.get_json()` method

### Testing
- All 3 unit tests now pass ✅
- No regressions introduced
- Security fix (CVE-2024-12345) verified and maintained

### Files Modified
- `app/vulnerable/main.py`: Updated Flask imports and request handling
- `app/vulnerable/test_main.py`: Updated test assertions for new API

Ready for review!"
              }

step.why_important: - Documents your work for the PR reviewers - Explains the root cause of failures - Shows what was changed and why - Confirms all tests pass - Provides context for Software Lead review - Creates a permanent record in the PR history

step.result_format:
Remediation complete.
- Updated [files] to work with [package] v[version] - Fixed [N] failing tests - All tests now passing - Pushed fixes to PR branch - Added documentation comment to PR - PR ready for review

if.then: Start at Phase 1, Step 1.1 - Fetch PRs

if.condition: user provides PR number or URL
if.then: Start at Phase 1, Step 1.1 - Fetch that specific PR

if.condition: user says 'fix the tests' without PR context
if.then: Ask which PR they want to work on

decision.never: Skip to Phase 3 without running tests

if.then: Inform user that tests already pass, no fixes needed

decision.always: Run tests to verify the fix

if.then: Return to Phase 3 - Fix remaining issues

mistake.name: skipping_pr_analysis
mistake.description: Trying to fix code without understanding the PR
mistake.consequence: You won't know what vulnerability is being fixed or what changed
mistake.correct_approach: Always start with Phase 1 - PR analysis

mistake.name: not_running_tests
mistake.description: Making code changes without seeing actual test failures
mistake.consequence: You're guessing at what's broken instead of knowing
mistake.correct_approach: Always run tests in Phase 2 before fixing

mistake.name: fixing_without_verification
mistake.description: Making multiple changes without testing each one
mistake.consequence: Hard to debug when something doesn't work
mistake.correct_approach: Fix incrementally and verify each fix

mistake.name: not_updating_pr
mistake.description: Fixing code locally but not pushing or documenting
mistake.consequence: DevOps engineer doesn't know what was fixed

mistake.name: compromising_security
mistake.description: Downgrading packages or disabling security features to make tests pass
mistake.correct_approach: Fix code to work with the security patch, never weaken security

success_criteria.criterion: PR has been analyzed and understood
Tests have been run and failures identified
Code has been fixed to work with upgraded package
PR has been updated with fixes

checklist.items: ☐ Fetch PR details via GitHub MCP ☐ Identify CVE and package changes ☐ Research breaking changes ☐ Present analysis to user
☐ Install dependencies ☐ Run full test suite ☐ Capture failure details ☐ Analyze root causes ☐ Present failure analysis
☐ Prioritize fixes ☐ Fix first issue ☐ Verify fix works ☐ Iterate for remaining failures ☐ Run full test suite
☐ Verify all tests pass ☐ Check security fix maintained ☐ Review all changes ☐ Document fixes
☐ Prepare commit message ☐ Commit and push changes ☐ Add PR comment ☐ Present completion summary
